REUTERS/Nathan Howard/File Photo <a data-testid="Link" target="_blank" referrerPolicy="no-referrer-when-downgrade" href="https://www.reutersconnect.com/item/us-president-trump-hosts-a-round-table-on-collegiate-sports-in-washington/dGFnOnJldXRlcnMuY29tLDIwMjY6bmV3c21sX1JDMjlaSkFBTjlQQQ%3D%3D/?utm_medium=rcom-article-media&utm_campaign=rcom-rcp-lead" rel="noopener" class="text-module__text__0GDob text-module__inherit-color__PhuPF text-module__inherit-font__1P1hv text-module__inherit-size__EyiQW link-module__link__INqxZ link-module__underline_default__-okuC link-module__with-icon__qlg76 collapsible-caption-module__license__CPQvV">Purchase Licensing<span><span class="link-module__suffix__ObiNY"> Rights</span><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 32 32" aria-hidden="true" focusable="false" role="presentation" data-testid="NewTabSymbol" class="link-module__new-tab-symbol__d53s7 link-module__move-left__CCZH6 collapsible-caption-module__symbol__943UJ" fill="#666" width="0" height="0"><path fill="#666" d="M15 6c.6 0 1 .4 1 1s-.4 1-1 1H9c-.6 0-1 .4-1 1v14a1.1 1.1 0 0 0 .2.6c.1.2.4.4.8.4h14c.6 0 1-.4 1-1v-6c0-.6.4-1 1-1s1 .4 1 1v6a3 3 0 0 1-3 3H9a3 3 0 0 1-2.2-.9A3 3 0 0 1 6 23V9a3 3 0 0 1 3-3Zm10 0 .4.1.5.5.1.4v6c0 .6-.4 1-1 1s-1-.4-1-1V9.4l-5.3 5.3a1 1 0 0 1-.7.3 1 1 0 0 1-.7-.3 1 1 0 0 1 0-1.4L22.6 8H19c-.6 0-1-.4-1-1s.4-1 1-1Z"></path></svg><span style="border:0;clip:rect(0 0 0 0);clip-path:inset(50%);height:1px;margin:-1px;overflow:hidden;padding:0;position:absolute;width:1px;white-space:nowrap">, opens new tab</span></span></a></span></div></figure></div><div class="article-body-module__content__bnXL1"><div data-testid="paragraph-0" class="text-module__text__0GDob text-module__dark-grey__UFC18 text-module__regular__qJJtA text-module__small__sph8i body-module__full_width__kCIGb body-module__small_body__gOmDf article-body-module__paragraph__Ts-yF">WASHINGTON, April 3 (Reuters) - U.S. President <a data-testid="Link" referrerPolicy="no-referrer-when-downgrade" href="https://www.reuters.com/world/us/donald-trump/" class="text-module__text__0GDob text-module__inherit-color__PhuPF text-module__inherit-font__1P1hv text-module__inherit-size__EyiQW link-module__link__INqxZ link-module__underline_default__-okuC">Donald Trump</a> issued an executive order on Friday that aims to reinforce the rules of college sports in a bid to restore ​financial stability and protect the future of college athletics.</div><div data-testid="paragraph-1" class="text-module__text__0GDob text-module__dark-grey__UFC18 text-module__regular__qJJtA text-module__small__sph8i body-module__full_width__kCIGb body-module__small_body__gOmDf article-body-module__paragraph__Ts-yF">Friday's order directs ‌federal agencies to bolster the effectiveness of key college-sports rules on transferring, eligibility and pay-for-play. It seeks evaluations on whether any violations of such rules render a university unfit for ​federal grants and contracts.</div><p data-testid="promo-box" class="text-module__text__0GDob text-module__dark-grey__UFC18 text-module__regular__qJJtA text-module__small__sph8i body-module__base__o--Cl body-module__small_body__gOmDf article-body-module__promo-box__hVl8h"> <!-- -->Sign up<!-- --> <!-- --> <a data-testid="Link" referrerPolicy="no-referrer-when-downgrade" href="undefined?location=article-paragraph&redirectUrl=%2Flegal%2Fgovernment%2Ftrump-issues-executive-order-bolster-college-sports-rules-2026-04-03%2F" class="text-module__text__0GDob text-module__inherit-color__PhuPF text-module__inherit-font__1P1hv text-module__inherit-size__EyiQW link-module__link__INqxZ link-module__underline_default__-okuC">here.</a></p><div data-testid="element" class="article-body-module__element__5eCce"></div><div data-testid="paragraph-2" class="text-module__text__0GDob text-module__dark-grey__UFC18 text-module__regular__qJJtA text-module__small__sph8i body-module__full_width__kCIGb body-module__small_body__gOmDf article-body-module__paragraph__Ts-yF">It calls on the relevant governing bodies to ​update rules by establishing clear eligibility limits, setting transfer rules as ⁠well as banning "improper" financial arrangements including pay-for-play agreements facilitated by collectives and similar ​entities.</div><div data-testid="paragraph-3" class="text-module__text__0GDob text-module__dark-grey__UFC18 text-module__regular__qJJtA text-module__small__sph8i body-module__full_width__kCIGb body-module__small_body__gOmDf article-body-module__paragraph__Ts-yF">The order also calls on Congress to quickly pass legislation to address these ​critical issues.</div><div data-testid="paragraph-4" class="text-module__text__0GDob text-module__dark-grey__UFC18 text-module__regular__qJJtA text-module__small__sph8i body-module__full_width__kCIGb body-module__small_body__gOmDf article-body-module__paragraph__Ts-yF">Trump issued an executive order in July that sought to block some recruiting payments by third parties to college athletes in big-money sports like football and men's basketball in ​order to preserve funds available for women's and non-revenue sports.</div><div data-testid="element" class="article-body-module__element__5eCce"></div><div data-testid="paragraph-5" class="text-module__text__0GDob text-module__dark-grey__UFC18 text-module__regular__qJJtA text-module__small__sph8i body-module__full_width__kCIGb body-module__small_body__gOmDf article-body-module__paragraph__Ts-yF">Trump said at a ​White House event a month ago that the soaring cost of college football programs was ‌hurting ⁠college sports in general, and vowed to issue a new executive order that would be more comprehensive.</div><div data-testid="paragraph-6" class="text-module__text__0GDob text-module__dark-grey__UFC18 text-module__regular__qJJtA text-module__small__sph8i body-module__full_width__kCIGb body-module__small_body__gOmDf article-body-module__paragraph__Ts-yF">The value of name, image and likeness - known as NIL - contracts has been rising, especially for football players, creating a costly burden for colleges ​that is forcing ​some of them ⁠to abandon some other sports, Trump said last month.</div><div data-testid="element" class="article-body-module__element__5eCce"></div><div data-testid="paragraph-7" class="text-module__text__0GDob text-module__dark-grey__UFC18 text-module__regular__qJJtA text-module__small__sph8i body-module__full_width__kCIGb body-module__small_body__gOmDf article-body-module__paragraph__Ts-yF">Until five years ago, the NCAA prohibited college athletes from accepting compensation ​for the use of their name, image and likeness. ​After a ⁠2021 Supreme Court ruling, the NCAA changed its rules to allow college athletes to be paid.</div><div data-testid="paragraph-8" class="text-module__text__0GDob text-module__dark-grey__UFC18 text-module__regular__qJJtA text-module__small__sph8i body-module__full_width__kCIGb body-module__small_body__gOmDf article-body-module__paragraph__Ts-yF">Although football and basketball attract by far the most attention among college sports, ⁠many ​non-football college athletes help feed teams for the ​Olympics.</div><div data-testid="paragraph-9" class="text-module__text__0GDob text-module__dark-grey__UFC18 text-module__regular__qJJtA text-module__small__sph8i body-module__full_width__kCIGb body-module__small_body__gOmDf article-body-module__paragraph__Ts-yF">College athletics support over half a million student-athletes with nearly $4 billion in scholarships annually and produced ​75% of the 2024 U.S. Olympic Team.</div><div class="article-body-module__element__5eCce"><p data-testid="SignOff" class="text-module__text__0GDob text-module__dark-grey__UFC18 text-module__regular__qJJtA text-module__extra_small__8Buss body-module__full_width__kCIGb body-module__extra_small_body__Bfz20 sign-off-module__text__LQAMP">Reporting by Humeyra Pamuk; Editing by Mark Porter</p></div><p data-testid="Body" dir="ltr" class="text-module__text__0GDob text-module__dark-grey__UFC18 Trump</a> issued an executive order on Friday that aims to reinforce the rules of college sports in a bid to restore ​financial stability and protect the future of college athletics.","type":"paragraph"},{"content":"Friday's order directs ‌federal agencies to bolster the effectiveness of key college-sports rules on transferring, eligibility and pay-for-play. It seeks evaluations on whether any violations of such rules render a university unfit for ​federal grants and contracts.","type":"paragraph"},{"content":"It calls on the relevant governing bodies to ​update rules by establishing clear eligibility limits, setting transfer rules as ⁠well as banning \"improper\" financial arrangements including pay-for-play agreements facilitated by collectives and similar ​entities.","type":"paragraph"},{"content":"The order also calls on Congress to quickly pass legislation to address these ​critical issues.","type":"paragraph"},{"content":"Trump issued an executive order in July that sought to block some recruiting payments by third parties to college athletes in big-money sports like football and men's basketball in ​order to preserve funds available for women's and non-revenue sports.","type":"paragraph"},{"content":"Trump said at a ​White House event a month ago that the soaring cost of college football programs was ‌hurting ⁠college sports in general, and vowed to issue a new executive order that would be more comprehensive.","type":"paragraph"},{"content":"The value of name, image and likeness - known as NIL - contracts has been rising, especially for football players, creating a costly burden for colleges ​that is forcing ​some of them ⁠to abandon some other sports, Trump said last month.","type":"paragraph"},{"content":"Until five years ago, the NCAA prohibited college athletes from accepting compensation ​for the use of their name, image and likeness. ​After a ⁠2021 Supreme Court ruling, the NCAA changed its rules to allow college athletes to be paid.","type":"paragraph"},{"content":"Although football and basketball attract by far the most attention among college sports, ⁠many ​non-football college athletes help feed teams for the ​Olympics.","type":"paragraph"},{"content":"College athletics support over half a million student-athletes with nearly $4 billion in scholarships annually and produced ​75% of the 2024 U.S. Olympic Team.","type":"paragraph"}],"promo_items":{"images":[{"url":"https://cloudfront-us-east-2.images.arcpublishing.com/reuters/XI6VDRIBMFNLJACPKDFBP3BGUU.jpg","caption":"U.S. President Donald Trump looks on during a round table on collegiate sports in the White House in Washington, D.C., March 6, 2026. News","videos":[{"id":"RW967503042026RP1","type":"video","subtype":"application/x-mpegURL","external_id":"RW967503042026RP1","title":"US judge tosses Blake Lively harassment case against Justin Baldoni","description":"A federal judge on Thursday dismissed actor Blake Lively's sexual harassment claims in her lawsuit against actor and director Justin Baldoni, significantly narrowing her case over the filming of their 2024 romantic drama \"It Ends With Us.\" Cara Angeline Oliver has more.","duration":92.5,"transcript_data":{"transcript":"A federal judge on Thursday threw out actor Blake Lively’s sexual harassment claims against actor and director Justin Baldoni.  The ruling comes after more than a year of legal wrangling over what happened during the production of the 2024 romantic drama, *It Ends With Us*, which was directed by Baldoni and starred both actors. Lively's lawyers argued Baldoni repeatedly crossed professional lines on set, accusing him of deviating from the script and adding what they described as unnecessary sexual content. That included one scene where he allegedly nuzzled Lively without consent. But in a 152-page decision, U.S. District Judge Lewis Liman said the alleged conduct appeared to be aimed at     Lively’s character and not Lively herself.  He said filmmakers must have room to experiment within the bounds of an agreed script without automatically facing sexual harassment liability.  Still, the case isn't over. Liman ruled jurors can hear Lively’s retaliation claim including whether Baldoni’s production company,  Wayfarer Studios, damaged her career after she complained about misconduct on set.   Jurors will not decide whether Lively was sexually harassed, but they may hear some of her allegations when the case goes to trial on May 18th. The actress's lawyer said she looks forward to testifying, while Baldoni's attorneys welcomed the ruling, calling the remaining case significantly narrower. Despite mixed reviews, It Ends With Us went on to gross more than $350 million worldwide."},"published_time":"2026-04-03T03:24:43Z","updated_time":"2026-04-03T03:24:47Z","thumbnail":{"url":"https://ajo.prod.reuters.tv/api/v2/img/69cf325ee4b0b60af064a28f-1775186687511","caption":"A federal judge on Thursday dismissed actor Blake Lively's sexual harassment claims in her lawsuit against actor and director Justin Baldoni, significantly narrowing her case over the filming of their 2024 romantic drama \"It Ends With Us.\" Cara Angeline Oliver has more.","type":"image","resizer_url":"https://ajo.prod.reuters.tv/api/v2/img/69cf325ee4b0b60af064a28f-1775186687511"},"source":{"hls":"https://ajo.prod.reuters.tv/v3/playlist/759571/master.m3u8"},"channels":[{"id":"13978","name":"US News","regions":["US"]},{"id":"189f8813eb619acad6694531e1004d4fcae3c097","name":"Entertainment","regions":["US"]},{"id":"HPUS","name":"RCOM Homepage US","regions":["US"]},{"id":"legal_news","name":"Legal News","regions":["US"]}],"advert_info":{"advert_params":[{"key":"adfriendly","value":true}],"advert_zone":"/4735792/reuters.com"},"meta_data":[{"key":"link","value":"tag:reuters.com,2026:newsml_RW967503042026RP1:4"},{"key":"byline","value":"NEW YORK CITY, NEW YORK, UNITED STATES"},{"key":"hidden","value":"false"},{"key":"region","value":"Remote"},{"key":"dateline","value":"Reuters, Reuters, APR 03"},{"key":"lmp-feed","value":"lp"},{"key":"copyright","value":"(c) 2026 Thomson Reuters, unless otherwise identified. Romero said he was \"confident that we will win this case and that the future of this country will be determined by our Constitution.\"","duration":64.86,"transcript_data":{"transcript":"I come out of the court today with the thought of my parents and so many of our parents and ancestors who came to this country seeking refuge,seeking new opportunities, and who relied on the rule that we've had in this country for 150 years, that everyone born here is a United States citizen, all alike. And I am confident that the court is going to turn back this president's effort to radically rewrite our 14th Amendment rule of birthright citizenship. I was especially gratified that President Trump was sitting a mere six feet away from me, and I hope that he was schooled in the importance of birthright citizenship. I am confident that we will win this case and that the future of this country will be determined by our Constitution, by our civil rights statutes  and not by the whim of a president who wishes to wipe away hundreds of years of precedent."},"published_time":"2026-04-01T18:52:46Z","updated_time":"2026-04-01T18:52:47Z","thumbnail":{"url":"https://ajo.prod.reuters.tv/api/v2/img/69cd68fae4b01a8b78ae7a00","caption":"The American Civil Liberties Union's Executive Director Anthony Romero and its National Legal Director Cecillia Wang on Wednesday (April 1) spoke after the Supreme Court heard oral arguments over the legality of birthright citizenship, at which President Donald Trump made an historic visit. Blanch Ancla has more.","duration":90.9,"transcript_data":{"transcript":"A U.S. judge has temporarily blocked the Pentagon from blacklisting AI company Anthropic. Thursday's ruling marks the latest turn in the startup's dispute with the military over AI use on the battlefield.  Anthropic sued in California federal court after Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth labeled the company a national security  supply-chain risk.   Washington can apply that label to companies that expose military systems to potential infiltration or sabotage by adversaries.  The AI startup argued that Hegseth overstepped his authority with the designation.   It also said the government was retaliating against the company for its views on AI safety,  violating its First Amendment rights.    The designation followed Anthropic's refusal to allow its AI system, Claude, to be used for domestic surveillance or autonomous weapons. As a result, the company was blocked from certain military contracts. Executives say the decision could  cost billions in business and reputational damage.  Its designation was the first time a U.S. company has been publicly designated a supply-chain risk.  U.S. District Judge Rita Lin agreed with the firm. She said     the administration’s actions appeared aimed at punishing the company, rather than protecting national security.   The decision will take effect in seven days to allow time for an appeal.  Anthropic welcomed the ruling and said its focus remains on safe and reliable AI.  A separate lawsuit over another Pentagon designation is still pending in Washington."},"published_time":"2026-03-27T06:41:41Z","updated_time":"2026-03-27T06:41:42Z","thumbnail":{"url":"https://ajo.prod.reuters.tv/api/v2/img/69c62618e4b0c93aa2d92254","caption":"A U.S. judge on Thursday (March 26) temporarily blocked the Pentagon's blacklisting of Anthropic, the latest turn in the Claude maker's high-stakes fight with the military over AI safety on the battlefield. Olivia Zollino reports.","duration":95.6,"transcript_data":{"transcript":"Demonstrators for and against Nicolás Maduro rallied outside a Manhattan courthouse, where the ousted Venezuelan president and his wife, Cilia Flores, appeared for a hearing on Thursday... ...more than two months after U.S. military forces captured them in a surprise raid on Caracas and ferried them to New York. The judge questioned the U.S. government's justification for blocking Maduro from using Venezuelan funds to pay for his legal defense against drug trafficking charges, but signaled he would not dismiss the case on that basis. Maduro and Flores have pleaded not guilty to charges including narcoterrorism conspiracy and are jailed in Brooklyn pending trial. They had asked the judge to dismiss the charges, arguing that their inability to rely on Venezuelan public funds interferes with their right to a lawyer of their choosing. Prosecutors argue that because the U.S. has not recognized Maduro as Venezuela's legitimate president since 2019, he and Flores should not expect the U.S. government to allow Venezuela to pay their legal fees. The prosecutors say Maduro and Flores can be assigned public defenders if they cannot afford their own lawyers. The judge said he would not make a formal ruling on whether the Venezuelan government can pay the legal fees or the motion to dismiss the case given ongoing uncertainty around the funds and whether Maduro and Flores had other assets they could use. 'They've really sued him just in a fraction of the kind of things that he's done.' Earlier on Thursday, President Donald Trump said the U.S. will bring additional cases against Maduro, without offering details."},"published_time":"2026-03-26T19:43:09Z","updated_time":"2026-03-26T19:43:10Z","thumbnail":{"url":"https://ajo.prod.reuters.tv/api/v2/img/69c58be5e4b0c93aa2d9208d","caption":"A U.S. judge questioned on Thursday (March 26) the U.S. government's justification for blocking ousted Venezuelan president Nicolás Maduro from using the South American country's funds to pay for his legal defense against U.S. drug trafficking charges. Syakir Jasnee reports. ","duration":78.73,"transcript_data":{"transcript":"A Florida woman pleaded not guilty on Wednesday to the attempted murder of singer Rihanna. At a hearing in Los Angeles, the lawyer of 35-year-old Ivana Lisette Ortiz, entered a not guilty plea to the 14 charges she faces.   These include one count of attempted murder and 10 felony counts of assault with a semi-automatic firearm. Judge Theresa McGonigle rejected a request to reduce her bail, which is set at $1.875 million.   The judge also said Ortiz cannot work as a licensed speech therapist in California while the case is ongoing.   Deputy District Attorney Alexander Bott described the incident.   The defendant drove to the victim's residence, positioned her vehicle and fired approximately 20 rounds from an AR-style  rifle into residences where multiple adults and children were present. The record also reflects that this was captivated conduct. She brought a loaded rifle, ammunition and even a disguise in the form of a wig, which demonstrates planning.  The shooting lasted several seconds. It was directed into areas where people and children were present. No one was struck by the gunfire, and Ortiz fled the scene but was arrested shortly after. Bott also called her a flight risk because she lives in Florida and said the high bail was needed to protect the public. If convicted, Ortiz could face life in prison."},"published_time":"2026-03-26T03:04:45Z","updated_time":"2026-03-26T03:04:46Z","thumbnail":{"url":"https://ajo.prod.reuters.tv/api/v2/img/69c4a1e7e4b0c93aa2d91c90","caption":"A Florida woman pleaded not guilty on Wednesday (March 25) to the attempted murder of singer Rihanna after authorities said she fired gunshots at the Grammy-winning musician's Los Angeles mansion this month. Ryan Brooks reports.","duration":198.66,"transcript_data":{"transcript":"A landmark verdict Wednesday found Meta and Google liable for designing platforms dangerous to kids and teens. Both companies were slapped with damages to the tune of $6 million. The Los Angeles jury found Meta liable for $4.2 million of that and Alphabet's Google for $1.8 million. Those are small amounts for two of the world's most valuable companies...  but the decision could force Big Tech to rethink how it defends itself against safety claims. Parents and child safety advocates celebrated news of a verdict outside the court, \"This is the beginning of the tides turning on Big Tech, for sure.\" The case focused on a 20-year-old woman who said she became addicted to YouTube and Instagram  at a young age because of their attention-grabbing design. Plaintiffs focused on how social media platforms are designed, and not the content itself.  That made it much harder for the companies to be found not liable. The jury found both companies negligent in designing both YouTube and Instagram, and they failed to warn of their dangers. Snap and TikTok were also defendants, but both settled before the trial began.   Those terms weren't disclosed. The plaintiff's lawyer, Mark Lanier, said Wednesday's verdict bodes well for thousands of other, similar cases.  \"This is the tip of the spear. And you've got to remember, this is what's called a bellwether.   It's the first trial in the history of American jurisprudence;  it usually takes the plaintiffs three or four times to figure out how to win these cases.   The plaintiffs are supposed to lose the first three or four. We won.” Meta spokesperson Ashly Nikkole Davis gave a statement outside the court: \"We respectfully disagree with the verdict and will appeal.   Teen mental health is profoundly complex and cannot be linked to a single app. We will continue to defend ourselves vigorously as every case is different, and we remain confident in our record of protecting teens online.\" A Google company spokesperson said they plan to appeal.  Meta also said it plans to appeal. It all comes as tech companies face rising pressure over child and teen safety. The verdict could mark a turning point in the global backlash against their platforms' perceived mental health harms to kids and teens. Collin Walke, who leads Hall Estil's cybersecurity and data privacy law practice, said the verdict sets a precedent for other cases. \"I think it's been a long time coming  because it's not as though any of the facts that came out at trial were particularly surprising to the general public. We've known for at least a decade now with tell-all books for former Google and Meta employees who come out and say, hey, we're using casino-style  techniques in our algorithms to try and keep children on our screens so that we can sell more products.  So it's not a particular surprise, long time coming much like the tobacco litigation,  so, I think the  jury's verdict will ultimately be upheld.\" More, separate trials in the U.S. are set for the months ahead. Another major trial involving several states and school districts is set for this summer in Oakland, California. A separate Los Angeles trial begins in July."},"published_time":"2026-03-25T23:50:18Z","updated_time":"2026-03-25T23:50:19Z","thumbnail":{"url":"https://ajo.prod.reuters.tv/api/v2/img/69c473d6e4b0c93aa2d91c31","caption":"Alphabet's Google and Meta were found liable on Wednesday (March 25) for designing platforms that are dangerous for kids and teens, in a landmark verdict that could force tech firms to rethink how they defend themselves against safety claims. The plaintiff's lawyer Mark Lanier said Wednesday's verdict \"bodes well for the other thousands of cases that are out there.\"","duration":82.4,"transcript_data":{"transcript":"\"There are so many families who've been tragically hurt through the addiction of social media. And while we've never been able to talk about the content  of what these social media platforms can do,  that's protected under the judge's rulings, we have been able to talk about the features.  And we've sent a message with this that you will be held accountable for the features.\"  \"And this is the tip of the spear. And you've got to remember, this is what's called a bellwether, it's the first trial. In the history of American jurisprudence it usually takes the plaintiffs three or four times to figure out how to win these cases. The plaintiffs are supposed to lose the first three or four. We won and so that's provocative in what it says to everyone.  You had Google's A Team here. You had Metta's A Team here. They had the best lawyers that their money could buy   and they went after us and went after poor Kaley and her family like vultures over a carcass. I mean, they were all  over everything and they didn't win. Washington said the move was not sufficient to lift its recent designation of Kabul as a \"state sponsor of wrongful detention\" and called for it to take ​more action. Kristy Kilburn reports.","duration":145.4,"transcript_data":{"transcript":"Dennis Coyle touched American soil for the first time in more than a year on Wednesday, after his release from Taliban detention in Afghanistan a day earlier. The 64-year-old had a tearful reunion with his family at Joint Base San Antonio in Texas. Coyle’s sister, Molly Long, called it a “joyful day” and thanked members of the U.S. government for their role in her brother’s return. 'We have special thanks for President Trump and his strength and tenacity to bring Dennis as the 105th hostage home under his tutelage.' 'We appreciate that. Secretary Rubio, Sebastian Gorka, Adam Boehler, we can't thank you enough, the state department, for all your help and support.' 'There's been so many people praying and supporting our family, and from the bottom of our hearts, we thank everybody for this day.' 'We are going to take some time as a family to heal, to spend special time with Dennis.' Coyle was taken from his home in Kabul in January 2025 and held without charges in near-solitary confinement. That's according to a senior U.S. official, who said Coyle's release followed direct action led by Secretary of State Marco Rubio,  and credited the persistence of Coyle's three sisters, whose advocacy helped drive the effort. The Taliban foreign ministry said in a statement that Afghan authorities had received a letter from Coyle’s mother requesting her son be pardoned and released on the occasion of the Islamic festival, Eid al-Firtr. The country’s Supreme Court then \"deemed the period of his detention sufficient and decided to release him.\" But Washington said Coyle’s release was not enough to lift its recent designation of Kabul as a “state sponsor of wrongful detention,” with Rubio saying, “while this is a positive step by the Taliban, more work needs to be done.” He also called for the immediate return of “all other unjustly detained Americans” and that, \"The Taliban must end their practice of hostage diplomacy.\" The Taliban’s foreign minister said Afghanistan did not detain citizens of any country for political purposes but over violations of its laws and that releases followed the completion of judicial procedures."},"published_time":"2026-03-25T16:50:21Z","updated_time":"2026-03-25T16:50:21Z","thumbnail":{"url":"https://ajo.prod.reuters.tv/api/v2/img/69c411d1e4b0c93aa2d91940","caption":"Dennis Coyle, who was detained by the Afghan Taliban government for more than a year, returned to the United States on Wednesday (March 25) after being released the day before. Ram Nabong reports.","duration":86.93,"transcript_data":{"transcript":"Minnesota sued the Federal Government on Tuesday, seeking access to evidence in shootings by Federal officers during an  immigration enforcement search in the state. Killed that lady right there in that car y'all The incidents include the fatal shooting of Renee Good and Alex Pretti, and a separate shooting that left another man injured.   The lawsuit alleges that the Department of Justice and the Department of Homeland Security blocked state investigators from key  evidence and information. In late January, following the killing of Pretti, a court granted the state a temporary restraining order ​barring federal investigators ​   from ⁠destroying evidence in the investigation. The restraining order was eventually dissolved by the judge who granted it. A spokesperson for the DHS said in a statement that all shootings are initially reviewed by an appropriate law enforcement agency. That is followed by an independent review within Immigration and Customs Enforcement or Customs and Border Protection. The spokesperson said the shooting of Good remains under review. According to the spokesperson, the FBI is leading the probe into the shooting of Brady, supported by Homeland Security  Investigations, with CBP also conducting its own internal investigation. The spokesperson said a joint review by ICE and DHS into the third case, the shooting of Venezuelan immigrant    Julio Cesar Sosa-Celis, found two officers may have given false statements. The statement said the officers are on leave pending an internal investigation, and could lose their jobs."},"published_time":"2026-03-25T06:06:51Z","updated_time":"2026-03-25T06:06:51Z","thumbnail":{"url":"https://ajo.prod.reuters.tv/api/v2/img/69c37ae1e4b0c93aa2d916d4","caption":"Minnesota sued the federal government on Tuesday (March 24), seeking access to evidence in the fatal shootings by federal officers of Renee Good and Alex Pretti and a shooting that injured another man during an immigration enforcement surge in the state, according to a complaint. Kristy Kilburn reports.","duration":60.4,"transcript_data":{"transcript":"U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth fired Army Chief of Staff Randy George on Thursday, the latest purge among the Pentagon’s most senior ranks. That's according to two defense officials and a source familiar with the matter who spoke to Reuters. The Pentagon confirmed that George, who had more than a year left in his term, will be retiring effective immediately. The department did not give a reason for George’s departure but said in a statement it was grateful for his decades of service. An infantry officer who served in Iraq and Afghanistan, George was confirmed to the top Army post in 2023. Terms in the role usually run for four years. Prior to the job, he was the vice chief of the Army and before that, the senior military adviser to then-Defense Secretary, Llyod Austin. The office for George did not immediately respond to a request for comment. His departure comes as the U.S. military builds up its forces in the Middle East while carrying out operations against Iran."},"published_time":"2026-04-02T23:26:03Z","updated_time":"2026-04-02T23:26:05Z","thumbnail":{"url":"https://ajo.prod.reuters.tv/api/v2/img/69cefa89e4b01a8b78ae7f17","caption":"U.S. Army Chief of Staff Randy George was fired on Thursday (April 2) by Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth, two U.S. ‌defense officials and a source familiar with the matter told Reuters, in the latest purge among the Pentagon's most senior ranks.
